project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve integrated urban management, service delivery, and disaster emergency management in Western Area and secondary cities of Sierra Leone
project_overview.total_financing: US$56.73 million (IDA: US$50.00 million; GEF: US$6.73 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 1.7 million people in eight local councils (seven cities and one rural district council): Freetown, Western Area Rural District, Makeni, Koidu New Sembehun, Kenema, Bo, Port Loko, and Bonthe

geographic_scope.coverage_type: Multi-city national coverage with focus on Western Area (Freetown) and secondary cities
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - Sierra Leone emerged from 11-year civil war (1991-2002) and remains fragile with persistent vulnerability to multidimensional shocks (epidemic, economic, climatic), North-South ethno-regional divide, high youth unemployment, weak institutions, and political polarization along ethnic lines. However, the country is not currently in active conflict.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Institutional and Capacity Development in Integrated Urban Management
key_components.description: Support for strengthening integrated urban planning, spatial data infrastructure, and property tax administration systems in city councils. Includes preparation of spatial plans, building control functions, municipal spatial data infrastructure (MSDI), and modernization of property tax systems to enhance own-source revenue generation.
key_components.financing: US$5.40 million (IDA: US$4.80 million; GEF: US$0.60 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Resilient Municipal Infrastructure Investment and Urban Greening
key_components.description: Financing for priority resilient municipal infrastructure including neighborhood upgrading in Freetown (drainage, roads, water/sanitation, street lighting), solid waste management (SWM) upgrading with modern landfill development, market upgrading in secondary cities, and large-scale urban tree planting and forest restoration.
key_components.financing: US$42.00 million (IDA: US$36.20 million; GEF: US$5.80 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Emergency Management Institutional and Capacity Development
key_components.description: Building capacity of national and local governments in emergency preparedness and response. Includes support to newly established National Disaster Management Agency (NDMA), establishment of Emergency Operations Centers (EOCs), strengthening early warning systems through meteorological and hydrological services, training in disaster risk management, and a Contingent Emergency Response Component (CERC) for immediate response to eligible crises.

Financing for project management unit (PMU) staffing, monitoring and evaluation, audits, safeguards management, financial management, procurement, grievance redress mechanism, and knowledge exchange activities.
key_components.financing: US$4.80 million (IDA: US$4.47 million; GEF: US$0.33 million)

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Finance (Fiscal Decentralization Division) hosts the Project Management Unit (PMU). Fiduciary management handled by Project Fiduciary Management Unit (PFMU) in MoF. Technical Coordination Agencies: Ministry of Local Government and Rural Development (MoLG), Ministry of Lands, Housing and Country Planning (MoLH), and National Disaster Management Agency (NDMA). Local implementation through City Project Implementation Teams (CPITs) in beneficiary local councils.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) comprising senior representatives from key ministries and agencies meets biannually for strategic oversight. Project Technical Committee (PTC) coordinates implementation across CPITs and TCAs. Ward Committees (WCs) serve as main interface with communities. Multi-stakeholder working groups in Freetown coordinate across sectors.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (2016, revised 2017-2020) and Sierra Leone National Public Procurement Act 2016. Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared. Use of World Bank Standard Procurement Documents. Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement (STEP) system for monitoring. Procurement risk rated Substantial with mitigation measures including capacity building, aggregation of packages, and private sector sensitization.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PMU establishes comprehensive M&E system with quarterly reports to World Bank within 45 days of quarter end. Annual assessments measure progress toward PDO. Independent annual E&S audits. Digital citizen engagement platform for real-time feedback. Mid-term and end-of-project beneficiary surveys. Results framework includes 24 indicators across PDO and intermediate results.

identified_risks.risk_description: Government reviewing major legislation (Local Government Act, Customary Land Rights Bill, Land Commission Bill) that could reassign functions targeted for strengthening at local council level. Delayed commitment to devolution of built-environment functions. Upcoming elections with mayors in five of seven beneficiary cities from opposition party, including Freetown where bulk of investments focused. Reliance on central government to finalize land purchase for landfill site.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Clear project budget allocation and implementation arrangements. Active technical support to LGA review process. Engagement with all political stakeholders. Coordination with prior actions of other World Bank projects.

identified_risks.risk_description: Rapid decline in fiscal space could impede delivery of critical infrastructure and services. Economy contracted 2.2% in 2020 due to COVID-19. Fiscal deficit doubled to 6.2% of GDP. Public debt at 73.6% of GDP with high risk of debt distress per IMF/World Bank analysis. Reduced fiscal space and exchange rate risk could affect urbanization programs.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project fully grant-financed (no debt increase). Government commitment to fiscal discipline demonstrated through implementation of fiscal measures and IMF Extended Credit Facility program. Comprehensive arrears clearance strategy based on debt transparency principles.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Limited technical capacity of local councils and national stakeholders in urban and DRM sectors. Large number of stakeholders requiring coordination with overlapping responsibilities at national and local levels. Weak capacity to implement projects of this scale. Coordination challenges between multiple agencies.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Capacity-strengthening activities in Components 1a and 1b specifically designed to address weaknesses. Project Technical Committee provides platform for regular coordination. FCC working groups support coordination in Freetown. Centralized PMU with dedicated staff. Consultant support for implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Limited capacity of PFMU staff; inadequate staffing compared to workload; limited knowledge of World Bank Procurement Regulations and STEP system; lack of procurement record keeping system; inefficiencies and delays in procurement; insufficient competition; weak complaint redress system.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Capacity building through regular procurement clinics and training. Hiring of additional procurement specialist for project. Aggregation of small packages when feasible. Sensitization of private sector to bid for tenders. Biannual reporting on complaints and actions taken. Regular FM supervision missions. Implementation of accounting software (TOMPRO). Internal audit oversight. External audits by independent auditors acceptable to World Bank.

identified_risks.risk_description: High E&S risk due to large scale and complex nature of interventions, sensitivity of recipients, significant social and environmental impacts, multiple implementing agencies with limited ESF experience, cumulative impacts on safety and well-being in informal settlements, impacts on biodiversity and sensitive ecosystems (forest reserves, wetlands). Specific risks include: occupational health and safety from labor influx; SEA/SH risks; disease spread including COVID-19; solid/liquid waste management; noise, dust, air pollution; water pollution; land acquisition and involuntary resettlement; potential displacement in densely populated neighborhoods; SWM disposal risks; tree planting sustainability; market upgrading disruptions.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preparation and implementation of comprehensive ESF instruments (ESMF, RPF, SEP, ESCP). Site-specific ESIAs, ESMPs, and RAPs before construction. COVID-19 Prevention and Risk Management Plan. GBV Action Plan and specialized NGO support. Labor Management Procedures. Codes of Conduct for contractors. Gender and GBV-sensitive GRM. Community engagement and consultation. Capacity building for PMU E&S staff. Third-party monitoring. Adherence to World Bank Good Practice Notes on Labor Influx, GBV, and SEA/SH. Disability inclusion measures.

identified_risks.risk_description: Incomplete devolution has left local councils with limited means and capacity. Functional responsibilities vaguely delineated between national and subnational governments. Delays in transferring powers and resources to local governments. Lack of defined policies on land use and public investment management.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recent Devolution Directive (March 7, 2019) and ongoing LGA review offer opportunities for clarifying responsibilities. Project provides TA to support devolution process. Coordination with government's decentralization agenda.

identified_risks.risk_description: Complexity of multi-sectoral project design. Coordination challenges across multiple components and agencies. Implementation in post-conflict context with weak institutional capacity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Technical design based on global experience and international best practices. Informed by lessons learned from previous operations (FERP). Feasibility studies and detailed designs to be completed before implementation. Centralized PMU with technical expertise. Regular World Bank implementation support missions.

identified_risks.risk_description: Need for sustained engagement with multiple stakeholders at national and local levels. Community participation and buy-in critical for sustainability. Potential resistance to changes in planning and revenue systems.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) prepared and disclosed. Intensive community consultations during preparation. Citizen engagement platforms for feedback. Ward Committees and community organizations involved in planning and implementation. Annual stakeholder surveys. Multi-stakeholder working groups in Freetown.

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-wide GRM to be established within 60 days of effectiveness. Multiple channels for complaints accessible to all stakeholders. Digital platform for documentation and tracking. Gender and GBV-sensitive design with confidential process for SEA/SH complaints. GBV service provider to support process. Referral pathway to service providers. Coordination with World Bank's corporate Grievance Redress Service and Inspection Panel available.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant gender focus addressing multiple WBG Gender Strategy pillars: (1) Removing constraints for more and better jobs - SWM livelihood support program for women, formal employment opportunities in waste sorting, short-term jobs in tree planting, childcare in markets; (2) Enhancing women's voice and agency - ensuring functional Ward Committees with statutory gender representation (5 of 10 members), women leadership roles, FGDs to identify women's infrastructure priorities, safety-enhancing infrastructure (street lighting), GBV prevention measures; (3) Removing barriers to asset ownership - including questions on women's property ownership in property tax surveys. Gender indicators include: women completing SWM livelihood program (target 800), percentage of formal SWM jobs for women (target 40%), female traders using upgraded markets (target 6,000 of 7,000), female beneficiaries of infrastructure (target 850,000 of 1,700,000).
vulnerable_groups: Women and girls (high rates of GBV, economic disadvantages, limited land ownership)
Youth (70% underemployment, skills gaps from civil war)
People with disabilities (4.3% of population, lack of accessibility, vulnerability in disasters)
Informal waste workers and waste pickers (many women, unsafe working conditions)
Residents of informal settlements (poor living conditions, flood/landslide risk, limited services)
Market traders (predominantly women, poor working conditions)
Poor and vulnerable households (disproportionately affected by disasters and COVID-19)
Persons affected by resettlement (in neighborhood upgrading and landfill sites)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project designed with conflict sensitivity considerations: (1) Addresses underlying fragility factors through institutional strengthening and service delivery improvements; (2) Focuses on inclusive urban management and decentralization to address governance constraints; (3) Emphasizes community participation and Ward Committees to build social cohesion; (4) Targets poor and vulnerable populations to reduce inequality and grievances; (5) Includes GBV prevention and response measures; (6) Supports disaster preparedness to reduce vulnerability to shocks that can trigger conflict; (7) Promotes revenue generation to reduce resource competition; (8) Engages all political stakeholders despite opposition control of some cities.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Sierra Leone remains fragile despite post-conflict progress: (1) Persistent vulnerability to multidimensional shocks (epidemic, economic, climatic) - experienced Ebola (2014-16), mining collapse (2015-16), Freetown landslide (2017), COVID-19 pandemic; (2) North-South ethno-regional divide with political polarization along ethnic lines; (3) High youth unemployment (70% underemployment) and inequality creating grievances; (4) Weak institutions and governance with corruption undermining state legitimacy; (5) Inadequate basic services (health, education, WASH) limiting human capital; (6) High GBV rates indicating social fragmentation; (7) Macroeconomic vulnerabilities (volatile growth, fiscal deficits, debt burden, high debt distress risk); (8) Limited disaster preparedness despite high exposure to natural hazards.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security considerations integrated throughout project: (1) COVID-19 Prevention and Risk Management Plan required within 60 days of effectiveness; (2) SEA/SH risks identified and mitigation measures including contractor codes of conduct, GBV-sensitive GRM, specialized NGO support; (3) Labor influx management to prevent social tensions and disease spread; (4) Community health and safety measures during construction; (5) Occupational health and safety plans for workers; (6) Disaster preparedness and early warning systems to reduce vulnerability; (7) Emergency Operations Centers and response capacity building; (8) Coordination with security agencies (Office of National Security) on DRM.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Limited but significant displacement anticipated: (1) Neighborhood upgrading in three densely populated informal settlements (Coconut Farm, Moyiba, Rokupa) with ~45,000 residents - in situ upgrading prioritized to minimize resettlement but some physical and economic displacement expected; (2) Landfill site development in Western Area - preliminary assessment suggests minimal houses but land acquisition and crop impacts anticipated; (3) Market upgrading may cause temporary displacement of livelihood activities during construction; (4) Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed (March 3, 2021); (5) Site-specific Resettlement Action Plans (RAPs) and Livelihood Restoration Plans to be prepared before construction; (6) Particular attention to livelihoods of women waste pickers potentially affected by future dumpsite closure; (7) Project funds secured for PAP compensation to avoid implementation delays; (8) Local-level resettlement working groups to support implementation.
